html:
Traumatic Brain Injury

Recoup's Integrative Root-Cause Rehabilitation Program for Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI)

Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) is a highly prevalent, debilitating, and often under-recognized public health crisis. Globally, over 69 million individuals sustain a TBI each year, making it one of the leading causes of death and disability across all age groups (Dewan et al., 2018). In India, the burden is especially high, with over 1.5 to 2 million new TBIs annually, largely due to road traffic accidents, falls, assaults, and occupational injuries (ICMR-NICPR, 2020). Alarmingly, TBI accounts for nearly 30% of trauma-related hospital admissions in India and is a growing cause of long-term neurological disability in the working population.

While traditionally viewed as a focal mechanical injury, TBI is now understood as a chronic, multi-system neurological disorder. The initial mechanical insult triggers a prolonged cascade of secondary injury mechanisms, including (Ekmark-Lewén et al., 2010; Werner & Engelhard, 2007; Ziebell & Morganti-Kossmann, 2010; Mayer et al., 2019):

  • Neuroinflammation and microglial activation
  • Excitotoxicity and glutamate-mediated neuronal death
  • Mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress
  • Blood-brain barrier (BBB) disruption and neurovascular uncoupling
  • Neuroendocrine imbalances, including HPA axis suppression
  • Gut-brain axis disruption and systemic immune activation

Most current rehabilitation paradigms focus on symptom control (e.g., pain, dizziness) and compensatory strategies. However, they often overlook the persistent root-cause physiological imbalances that prevent complete recovery and contribute to long-term consequences like chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE), neuropsychiatric syndromes, and early-onset neurodegeneration.

At Recoup Health, TBI is not viewed as a single-episode event, but rather as a chronic system disruption involving the brain, body, and behavior. Our 4R Integrative Root-Cause Rehabilitation Protocol (Relief, Root Cause Redressal, Restoration, and Regeneration) is designed to:

  • Address the biochemical, hormonal, and neuroimmune disruptions post-TBI
  • Repair brain-gut-microbiome communication
  • Restore autonomic nervous system balance and neurovascular coupling
  • Leverage neuroplasticity and regenerative medicine to enable lasting recovery
  • Provide precision rehabilitation tailored to each patient's clinical phenotype and lab profile

Our Team

  • Rehabilitation Physicians
  • Orthopedic Surgeon
  • Regenerative Medicine Physicians
  • Functional and Lifestyle Medicine Physicians
  • Ayurveda Physician
  • Naturopathy and Yoga Physicians

  • Occupational Therapists
  • Aquatic Therapists
  • Speech Therapists
  • Tai Chi and Qi Gong Therapists
  • Alexander and Feldenkrais Teachers
  • Orthotist and Prosthetist

  • Nutritionists

  • Nurses
  • Patient Care Coordinators
  • Other professionals, as required

Our approach is grounded in evidence-based strategies from functional medicine, neurorehabilitation, regenerative therapies, and mind-body science, with a deep emphasis on interdisciplinary collaboration and continuous outcome monitoring.

R1: Relief of Symptoms

Headaches, dizziness, fatigue, irritability, sleep disturbances, sensitivity to light and sound, cognitive impairment, and emotional dysregulation are characteristic of the early phase of TBI. This phase aims to stabilize neurological symptoms, reduce inflammation, prevent secondary complications, and support initial functional independence. Suggested Interventions:

  1. Neuro-education: Patient and family training on TBI recovery phases, pacing strategies, neuroplasticity, energy budgeting, and cognitive fatigue management.
  2. Pharmacotherapy: Targeted medications for headaches (amitriptyline, topiramate), mood (SSRIs, SNRIs), sleep (melatonin, trazodone), spasticity (baclofen), and sensory sensitivity.
  3. Vestibular and Oculomotor Therapy: Customized rehabilitation to restore gaze stability, reduce dizziness, improve balance, and enhance depth perception and convergence.
  4. Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) enhances processing speed, memory, naming, and expressive and receptive language through functional communication strategies.
  5. Occupational Therapy (OT): Focuses on activities of daily living (ADLs), executive function routines, sensory integration, and adaptive technology to promote independence.
  6. Physical Therapy (PT): Includes posture correction, cervical stabilization, proprioceptive training, and balance re-education under symptom thresholds.
  7. Craniosacral Therapy and Myofascial Release: Supports the recalibration of the autonomic nervous system and reduces head and neck tension. Promotes cerebrospinal fluid flow and relief from somatic tension patterns post-injury.
  8. Tai Chi, Qi Gong, and Breath-Integrated Movement: Promotes postural control, vagal activation, and emotional centering.
  9. Aquatic Therapy: Provides buoyancy-supported rehabilitation for proprioceptive deficits, balance training, and neuromuscular re-coordination.
  10. CBT and EMDR: Psychological support for emotional dysregulation, PTSD, grief, and anxiety symptoms related to trauma.
  11. Music and Art-Based Therapy: Engages right-brain neural pathways and helps modulate mood, enhance emotional expression, and reduce isolation.
  12. Sleep Optimization: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBTi), light/dark therapy, routine structuring, and melatonin rhythm support.
  13. Anti-inflammatory Nutrition: Neuroprotective, ketogenic-MIND hybrid diet rich in healthy fats, antioxidants, polyphenols, and gut-supporting prebiotics.
  14. Botulinum Toxin Injections, Orthopaedic Selective Spasticity Control Surgery (OSSCS): To relieve spasticity, which enables passive range of motion, prevents contracture formation, and improves tolerance to splinting and orthotic use.
  15. Hyperselective neurectomy (HSN) is a surgical procedure used to treat spasticity, particularly in the upper limbs, by selectively removing specific branches of peripheral nerves at the point where they enter muscles. This partial nerve removal aims to reduce spasticity while preserving the voluntary function of the hand.

R2: Root Cause Redressal

This phase investigates and targets the underlying drivers of prolonged post-concussion symptoms and chronic neuroinflammation, including mitochondrial dysfunction, neuroendocrine disruption, toxins, gut dysbiosis, and oxidative stress. Suggested Interventions include:

  1. Comprehensive Functional Testing: This includes hsCRP, homocysteine, fasting insulin, cortisol, thyroid panel, ApoE status, heavy metal analysis, mycotoxin testing, stool microbiome analysis, zonulin levels, organic acids test (OAT), and CoQ10 levels.
  2. Colon Hydrotherapy: Detoxification and microbiota support for patients with constipation, cognitive fog, or intestinal inflammation.
  3. Far Infrared Sauna (FIR): Enhances sweating-based detoxification, lymphatic drainage, and sympathetic downregulation; reduces toxin load and improves circulation.
  4. Near-Infrared Photobiomodulation: Stimulates mitochondrial ATP production, improves cerebral blood flow, and enhances cognitive function and emotional stability.
  5. Transcranial Pulsed Electromagnetic Field Therapy (PEMF Helmet): Applies low-intensity pulsed electromagnetic fields to stimulate cortical recovery, reduce neuroinflammation, and enhance microglial modulation.
  6. Mitochondrial Enhancement Therapy: IV and oral support with NAD+, CoQ10, ALA, acetyl-L-carnitine, magnesium threonate, and glutathione to restore cellular energy and antioxidant defenses.
  7. Vagal Restoration Therapy: HRV biofeedback, resonance breathing, cold exposure, chanting, and humming to enhance parasympathetic tone and brainstem recovery.
  8. Ayurveda Detoxification (Panchakarma): Basti (medicated enema), virechana (purgation), nasya (nasal therapy), and neurorestorative herbs like Brahmi, Ashwagandha, and Shankhpushpi.
  9. Neurotrophic Nutraceuticals: Curcumin, resveratrol, lion's mane, bacopa, phosphatidylserine, sulforaphane, and DHA for cognitive resilience and neuroprotection.
  10. Mind-Body Therapies: EMDR, EFT, DBT, and somatic therapies to reduce limbic dysregulation, trauma imprints, and chronic stress.
  11. Low-Dose Naltrexone (LDN) Therapy: Acts on microglial receptors to reduce neuroinflammation and improve pain, mood, and fatigue in chronic TBI syndromes.
  12. Customized Probiotics and Postbiotics: Based on stool tests, use targeted strains (e.g., Bifidobacterium longum, Lactobacillus rhamnosus) and postbiotics (butyrate, propionate) for brain–gut modulation.

R3: Restoration

This phase focuses on the reintegration of disrupted neurocognitive, sensorimotor, hormonal, and metabolic systems to promote adaptive functioning and long-term brain resilience. Interventions in this phase include:

  1. Coordination Dynamics Therapy (CDT) reorganizes cortical circuits through rhythmic, bilateral movement to enhance motor-cognitive integration and timing.
  2. Flint Rehab (FitMi, MusicGlove, and MiGo) helps TBI survivors regain lost abilities by motivating them to exercise impaired limbs daily. They achieve this through devices that offer engaging, gamified experiences to help restore motor function. These devices leverage neuroplasticity, the brain's ability to adapt and rewire itself, by encouraging high repetition of movements.
  3. Exergaming using Smart Playball: to improve upper body strength for hair washing, fine motor control for tying shoes, balance for wearing slip-ons, or cognitive tasks like language skills
  4. Neurofeedback and HRV Biofeedback: Enhances self-regulation of brainwave activity and autonomic balance to reduce cognitive overload, fatigue, and anxiety.
  5. Virtual and Augmented Reality: Engages dual-tasking, attention, executive processing, and visual-spatial retraining in immersive environments.
  6. Feldenkrais and Alexander Techniques: Improve postural awareness, movement control, and proprioceptive feedback through sensorimotor re-education.
  7. Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES): Re-establishes neuromuscular control in weakened or spastic limbs through patterned electrical input.
  8. Advanced Speech and Cognitive Therapy: Includes conversational scripting, narrative training, metacognitive strategy development, and compensatory cue systems.
  9. IV Micronutrient and Amino Acid Therapy: This therapy includes a B-complex, taurine, glutathione, magnesium, Vitamin C, and custom amino acid blends for supporting neurotransmitter synthesis.
  10. Endocrine Restoration: Support for adrenal, thyroid, and gonadal axes if dysregulated (e.g., adaptogens, hormone replacement as clinically appropriate).
  11. Occupational Therapy and Ergonomic Redesign: Helps reintroduce daily function, return to work, and manage executive fatigue through structured activity pacing.
  12. CGM-Based Glycemic Control: Identifies glycemic variability and promotes insulin sensitivity with personalized diet and exercise protocols.
  13. Latent Infections and Toxin Management: Chelation, antimicrobial therapy, and antifungal interventions for infections or bioaccumulated toxins delaying brain healing.
  14. Mirror Therapy: Uses visual illusion techniques to re-establish neural pathways in motor and emotional awareness circuits.
  15. Dynamic Gait and Balance Training with Sensory Feedback: Incorporates force plates, wearable sensors, balance boards, and treadmill-based dual-task walking. Improves cerebellar-cortical coordination, reactive balance, and reduces the risk of falls.
  16. Sensory Integration Therapy: Beneficial for individuals with sensory processing issues (hypersensitivity, tactile defensiveness). Uses graded exposure, vestibular swings, textures, and auditory modulation.

R4: Regeneration and Longevity

This phase targets long-term brain repair, regeneration, and resilience to reduce the risk of Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy, neurodegeneration, and premature aging. Suggested Interventions:

  1. Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT) promotes angiogenesis, stem cell proliferation, neurogenesis, and enhances oxygen delivery to dormant or ischemic brain areas.
  2. Peptide Therapies: Cerebrolysin, BPC-157, and Thymosin Beta-4
  3. IV Regenerative Nutrition: NAD+, glutathione, phosphatidylcholine, and amino acid-rich protocols for cellular maintenance and neuroimmune optimization.
  4. Autophagy and Amyloid Clearance: Intermittent fasting (16:8 or 18:6), as well as nutraceuticals such as quercetin, spermidine, and berberine, support brain cleanup and reduce residual inflammation.
  5. Molecular Hydrogen and Ozone Therapy: Used to reduce oxidative stress, improve mitochondrial efficiency, and support redox balance.
  6. Anti-Aging and Telomere Support: Interventions with omega-3, polyphenols, mitochondrial stabilizers, and circadian rhythm optimization.
  7. Frailty Index Monitoring: VO2 max, grip strength, HRV, gait speed, and cognitive tests (MoCA/CNS-VS) are used to track recovery and assess the risk of decline.
  8. Relapse Prevention Planning: Digital follow-up via Recoup EPHR, telemonitoring, emergency support protocols, caregiver training, and environmental adjustments.
  9. Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor (BDNF) Modulation: via Aerobic exercise (Zone 2 training), Lion's mane mushroom (hericenones), Cold exposure and heat stress (contrast therapy), SSRIs (low dose), magnesium L-threonate. BDNF is vital for synaptic plasticity, long-term memory, and neuronal survival.

Recoup Health's Credentials

Dr. Deepak Sharan brings over 25 years of international clinical experience in the rehabilitation and long-term care of neurological trauma, including mild, moderate, and severe Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI). He is a world-renowned Consultant in Orthopaedic Surgery, Rehabilitation, Orthopaedic Rheumatology, Pain, Musculoskeletal, Regenerative, Functional and Lifestyle Medicine, Ergonomics, and Occupational Health.

He is a Diplomate of the International Board of Lifestyle Medicine (Dip IBLM) and a Certified Functional Medicine Physician, trained by both the Institute for Functional Medicine (USA) and the Functional Medicine Academy (USA). He holds an advanced Certificate in Anti-Aging and Precision Medicine from the American Academy of Anti-Aging Medicine.

Dr. Sharan has undergone advanced clinical training at the Kharrazian Institute (USA) in key areas related to brain injury recovery, including Neuroinflammation, Traumatic Brain Injury, Post-Concussion Syndrome, Neuroendocrine Dysfunction, Cognitive Rehabilitation, Chronic Fatigue and Pain Syndromes, and Longevity and Performance Medicine.

He is also trained in the Wahls Protocol, developed by Dr. Terry Wahls, which emphasizes mitochondrial repair, nutritional neuroscience, and restoration of the gut-brain-immune axis—all critical pillars in post-TBI recovery.

He is trained in Orthopaedic Selective Spasticity Control Surgery (OSSCS) by its developer, Dr Takashi Matsuo, an Orthopedic Surgeon from Japan. He is trained in Hyperselective neurectomy (HSN) at the Mayo Clinic, Rochester, USA.

At Recoup Health, Dr. Sharan leads a highly specialized, multidisciplinary team that he has trained. This team-based, precision approach delivers a comprehensive, personalized, and root-cause-focused program for TBI rehabilitation, extending care from early concussion recovery to long-term cognitive regeneration and neuroprotection.
